problem_summary: 2020 MCM/ICM Problem D (operations research): using event/passing data from a fictional soccer club ("Huskies") across a 38-game season, build a passing network, identify structural and dynamic indicators of successful teamwork, advise the coach, and generalize the framework to non-sport teams. This Outstanding paper (team 2006782) frames every task around a weighted passing network and an opponent-aware regression scoring model.
models_used: Weighted passing network as an 11x11 adjacency matrix (undirected, w_ij = number of passes), built/visualized in Gephi

Network graph-theory metrics: Total Links L (sum of degrees /2), Network Density rho = L / C(n,2) = L/55, Network Diameter D from topological distance d_ij = 1/w_ij, and weighted Clustering Coefficient C_w(i) (Ahnert et al. ensemble formula) averaged over players
K-means clustering on the topological-distance matrix to detect dyadic and triadic player configurations (sub-networks like (D1,M1,F2))

Five-indicator performance feature set: Coordination (gamma = avg clustering coeff), Distribution of contributions (phi = f*d, forward success rate x defense success rate), Tempo (50-ball passing time t_b), Flexibility (sigma_y, std dev of pass y-coordinates), Pressing (mean x-coordinate)

Evaluation model based on 'Adversarial Regression': linear score S = beta1*x1+...+beta5*x5 + C computed for BOTH Huskies and opponent, with weights constrained so that S_1 vs S_2 reproduces actual win/loss/draw outcomes (|S1-S2|<1 for draws)

Greedy Algorithm to search weight combinations beta_i, selecting the set minimizing 'LowestError' (sum of standard deviations across matches); final S = 0.4*gamma + 0.6*phi + 0.2*t_b + 0.1*sigma_y + 0.1*x_bar + C
Multi-scale network analysis: time scale (10-minute passes per interval; whole-game/whole-season L trend) and region scale (field divided into 8 zones, pass heatmap)
Strategy/formation analysis mapping formations (4-3-3, 4-4-2, 5-3-2) to indicator fitness; benchmark comparison against Italy 2012 Euro semi-final as a defensive-counterattack exemplar
Generalization model for non-sport teams: informational passing distance for coordination, leadership intensity I = sum(1/d_N), effective coordination over time E = sum(t_work)/t_total
key_techniques: Casting passes as a weighted undirected graph and reusing standard network-science measures (density, diameter via inverse-weight distance, weighted clustering) rather than inventing ad-hoc stats — grounded in cited literature (Clemente, Buldu/Guardiola Barcelona paper, Ahnert)

The 'Adversarial Regression' twist: scoring the opponent with the SAME model and constraining weights to reproduce real match results, so the regression is supervised by outcomes and inherently relative/competitive rather than absolute

Using a train/test split (first 33 matches train, last 5 test) and explicit data cleaning (adjusted matches 31/33, discarded match 32, normalized to zero-mean unit-variance) to give the weight-fitting credibility
Greedy search over discrete weight grids with a custom error functional (LowestError = sum of per-match score std dev) to pick interpretable weights
Multi-scale framing (player dyad/triad, 10-min, full game, full season, field region) that directly answers the prompt's explicit request to 'explore multiple scales'

Sensitivity analysis on two design choices: the draw threshold epsilon in [0,2] (showing weights stabilize for epsilon>1) and the pass-count for Tempo (10/30/50/70/90), justifying 50-ball as the 'most scientific' choice
Benchmarking against a real-world exemplar (Italy national team / 2012 Italy v Germany) to make abstract advice concrete and credible
why_it_won: "From a judge's view it wins on completeness and narrative coherence rather than mathematical depth. Every one of the four tasks is answered directly and in order, the network model is reused end-to-end (one model feeds Tasks 1-4), and the central modeling idea — scoring the opponent with the same equation and forcing the weights to reproduce actual win/loss/draw results — is a genuinely clever, problem-specific insight that reframes teamwork evaluation as inherently competitive. The team grounds its metric choices in real soccer-network literature (the Guardiola Barcelona Scientific Reports paper, Clemente's World Cup network study), ran a train/test split and two real sensitivity analyses, and translated results into concrete, actionable coaching advice (switch to 5-3-2 / 4-1-3-2, protect M2, attack the wings) plus a vivid annotated tactical diagram. It also nails the generalization task (Task 4) with three quantifiable analogies (leadership intensity, informational distance, effective coordination over time) where many teams hand-wave. The judge sees a team that read the data, used the right tools, and told a clean story coach-to-judge — that combination of correctness signals, domain literature, and communication is what earns Outstanding."
section_highlights: "Strong, conventional MCM structure: one-page Summary that states the method and result of each task; Assumptions+Justifications and a Notations table up front; per-task chapters with sub-headings; explicit Sensitivity, Strengths/Weaknesses, References, and Code appendix. Writing highlights: (1) the Summary names the actual techniques (Passing network, Adversarial Regression, Defensive Counterattack) as keywords, signaling the core contribution immediately; (2) heavy, well-captioned visualization — Gephi network graphs per match, topological-distance heatmap, flexibility scatterplots, pressing line charts, and an annotated color-coded tactical movement diagram (Figure 12) that makes the advice tangible; (3) each indicator is introduced with intuition, then a formula, then a figure, a clean three-beat pattern; (4) the weight equation is interpreted in plain language (Distribution most important because tied directly to the result, then Coordination, etc.), connecting math back to soccer meaning; (5) honest data-handling disclosure (adjusted/discarded matches noted with asterisks in the table). The English is rough in places (typos, garbled sentences) but the logical scaffolding and figures carry the reader."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The 'Adversarial Regression' name overstates the method — it is really an outcome-constrained linear regression with a greedy weight search, not adversarial learning in any formal sense; the weights are coarse (0.1 increments) and the C intercept is never reported

Treating the passing network as UNDIRECTED (w_ij=w_ji) discards passer->receiver direction, losing playmaker/receiver asymmetry that is central to soccer tactics — a real modeling simplification they acknowledge only implicitly

Manual data tampering: they 'artificially adjusted' Coordination for matches 31 and 33 and discarded match 32 for 'too large deviation' — convenient but weakens the objectivity of the fit and risks overfitting on only ~32 training rows

Several metrics are defined loosely or inconsistently: flexibility formula (eq 11) is written as a mean of deviations (which is ~0), not a true standard deviation; clustering-coefficient numbers (7.31 vs 7.62, and 'density 6.71') are implausible as defined ratios, suggesting reported values don't match the stated formulas
Mixed assumption that 'there is no difference between opponents' strength level' contradicts later benchmarking against FIFA-ranked teams and undercuts the opponent-aware scoring premise
Generalization (Task 4) indicators (leadership intensity, informational distance) are proposed but never quantified on data — the authors themselves flag low persuasiveness in Weaknesses
Tempo/flexibility/pressing rely on x,y pass coordinates whose normalization to a 0-100 field is assumed but not validated; small sample (38 games, 11 nodes) limits statistical power
reusable_patterns: Network-as-adjacency-matrix template for any 'who-interacts-with-whom' OR problem: build weighted graph, then report Total Links/degree, Density, Diameter (via inverse-weight distance), and weighted Clustering Coefficient — a ready menu of defensible, citable structural metrics

Outcome-supervised weighting: when you must combine several indicators into one score and have known results (win/loss, success/failure), constrain or fit the weights so the combined score reproduces those known outcomes, and pick weights minimizing a custom error functional via greedy/grid search — turns a subjective weighting into a justified one
Score BOTH sides with the same model to make an evaluation relative/competitive instead of absolute — directly applicable to any adversarial or comparative OR setting

Multi-scale answer pattern: when a prompt says 'explore multiple scales,' explicitly enumerate and analyze micro (dyad/triad), temporal (per-interval, per-game, per-season), and spatial (zones) views — a structural checklist that guarantees full coverage

Two-axis sensitivity analysis on your own design hyperparameters (here the draw threshold epsilon and the pass-count window), showing where results stabilize, to defend otherwise-arbitrary modeling choices

Benchmark-against-a-real-exemplar move: validate and contextualize recommendations by comparing the modeled team to a famous real case (here Italy's defensive counterattack), making abstract advice credible and concrete
Annotated tactical/flow diagram with a color legend to communicate a strategy recommendation visually — a high-impact, low-cost figure pattern for any 'give actionable advice' task
Summary-as-keywords discipline: lead with a Summary that names your actual methods so judges immediately grasp the contribution
